summary: My older brother was born in Tule Lake, and my parents were incarcerated there during World War II. My mother went into Tule Lake pregnant, which was a difficult experience given the harsh conditions and limited medical care. We visited Tule Lake recently to understand what they endured, realizing how hard it must have been for her to care for an infant in such a place. Tule Lake was a concentration camp where many Japanese Americans, including my family, were forcibly held after Pearl Harbor.

I was actually born in Caldwell, Idaho, after my parents left Tule Lake. They were sponsored by a farmer to work there, which was how they got out of the camp. Caldwell had a labor camp that was originally built for Dust Bowl migrants, but during the war, Japanese Americans like my parents worked in the sugar beet fields there. My father became a supervisor at the labor camp in Caldwell, and the place still exists today as low-income housing.

My parents got married on November 22, 1941, and just two weeks later, Pearl Harbor was attacked. That event changed everything for Japanese Americans, including my family, who began their married life in a concentration camp. Pearl Harbor was the catalyst for the forced relocation and incarceration of our community during the war.

My mother graduated from the University of Washington with a BA in Home Economics, which was quite an achievement at the time. Despite her degree, she was told by a college counselor that there were no jobs for her, especially as a woman of color. Her education and skills in sewing and design were important parts of her identity, even though opportunities were limited.

Before the war, the labor camp in Caldwell, Idaho, was created to house migrant workers from Arkansas and Oklahoma who were displaced by the Dust Bowl. These migrants moved west during the 1930s Dust Bowl and Great Depression seeking work and better living conditions. The camp later became a place where Japanese Americans, including my parents, worked during the war.

The Great Depression deeply affected many families, including those who moved west to places like Caldwell, Idaho, for work and housing. The labor camps were initially built as low-income housing for these Dust Bowl migrants struggling during the Depression. This economic hardship set the stage for the labor shortages that led to Japanese Americans being recruited for farm work during the war.

The Amalgamated Sugar Company played a key role in recruiting Japanese Americans from the camps to work in the sugar beet fields during World War II. They lobbied for workers because the war had created labor shortages, and farmers needed help harvesting crops. This company’s efforts allowed my parents and others to leave the camps and work in places like Caldwell, Idaho.

Michael Dittenber is the director of the labor camp site in Caldwell, Idaho, and he was very helpful in sharing the history with me and my family when we visited. He gave us a tour and explained the remaining buildings like the post office and water tower. His knowledge helped me understand the living conditions and the community that existed there during the war.

Robert Matsui and his family were also in the labor camp in Caldwell, Idaho, but they were fortunate because they did not have to return to Tule Lake. My mother remembered them being there, and it seemed like they had a somewhat different experience during that time. The Matsui family was part of the community of Japanese Americans working in the labor camps during the war.

Many young Japanese American men from places like San Francisco and Los Angeles were recruited to work in the sugar beet fields, even though many had no farming experience. My parents would chuckle about how these city boys didn’t even know what a hoe was when they started working. San Francisco was one of the urban centers where many Japanese Americans lived before the war and were uprooted.

Similar to San Francisco, Los Angeles was home to many young Japanese Americans who were sent to work in agricultural labor camps during the war. My parents noticed that many of these young men from Los Angeles had never done farm work before, which was quite a shock. Los Angeles was a major city where the Japanese American community was disrupted by the internment.

entity: Presidential Commission on Wartime Relocation and Internment of Civilians
summary: The Presidential Commission on Wartime Relocation and Internment of Civilians was a turning point for many Japanese Americans, including my family. It was only after this commission’s work that people felt it was okay to speak openly about their experiences. My parents and others began to share their stories, and the commission’s findings helped lead to the government’s formal apology and redress.

Grace Uyehara was a remarkable lobbyist in Washington who played a crucial role in the redress movement for Japanese Americans. She was a mover and shaker who brought together coalitions and worked tirelessly to get the government to apologize and provide reparations. Her efforts helped change the course of history for our community.

Judge Marutani was the only Japanese American member on the Presidential Commission on Wartime Relocation and Internment of Civilians. He was from Philadelphia and was part of a strong group of people there who believed in seeking justice and redress. His involvement was important in raising awareness and pushing the commission’s work forward.

Philadelphia was home to a strong Japanese American community that was very active in the redress movement. People like Grace Uyehara and Judge Marutani were based there, and the city became a hub for organizing and advocacy. Many Japanese Americans who resettled on the East Coast, including some from Seabrook Farms, made Philadelphia their home after the war.

Jim Wright was a Senator from Texas who supported the redress movement for Japanese Americans. His backing was significant because the 442nd Infantry Regiment, which rescued the Lost Battalion, had strong ties to Texas and were highly respected there. Getting his support helped broaden the coalition beyond the West Coast states.

Texas played an unexpected but important role in the redress movement because of its connection to the 442nd Infantry Regiment and the Lost Battalion rescue. Senator Jim Wright from Texas supported the cause, which helped gain political momentum. Texas’s respect for the Japanese American soldiers helped bridge regional divides during the redress campaign.

The 442nd Infantry Regiment was a highly decorated Japanese American unit during World War II, known especially for rescuing the Lost Battalion. Their bravery earned them respect across the country, including in Texas, which helped garner political support for redress. The regiment’s legacy is a source of pride for our community and helped change perceptions about Japanese Americans.

The Lost Battalion was a unit rescued by the 442nd Infantry Regiment during World War II, an event that brought great honor to Japanese American soldiers. This rescue was well-known and respected, particularly in Texas, and it helped build support for the redress movement. The story of the Lost Battalion symbolizes the courage and sacrifice of Japanese Americans during the war.

After the war, my family returned to Auburn, Washington, where my father worked in his brother-in-law's pottery business and became a supervisor. I grew up there, and it was a place where my parents could rebuild their lives after the camps. Auburn was home to our family before and after the war, and it shaped much of my early life.

Seabrook Farms was a place where many Japanese Americans were recruited to work after the war, and some of those families eventually resettled on the East Coast. It was part of the broader story of Japanese American dispersal and resettlement following incarceration. The Quakers and organizations like the American Friends Service Committee helped support these communities.

The American Friends Service Committee, a Quaker organization, was very helpful in supporting Japanese Americans after the war. They helped find housing and educational opportunities for Nikkei who were qualified to attend college. A close friend of mine worked for them, and their efforts made a real difference in rebuilding lives after incarceration.

Both of my parents were Nisei, second-generation Japanese Americans, and their experiences growing up were shaped by their small communities. My mother grew up in Burlington, Washington, where there were only three Japanese families, and they assimilated well because they were not seen as a threat. The Nisei generation faced unique challenges, balancing cultural identity and assimilation during a difficult era.

My mother grew up in Burlington, Washington, a small town where only three Japanese families lived. They were middle class and integrated well into the community, with my uncles participating in local activities like the band and class leadership. Burlington was very different from the West Coast cities where Japanese Americans faced more discrimination and economic threats.

Pendleton, Oregon, was mentioned as one of the places where Japanese Americans were relocated during the war. It was part of the network of camps and labor sites where families like mine were sent. Pendleton represents one of the many small towns affected by the wartime relocation policies.
